Grous notes Netflix itself has long framed its competition as everything from TikTok and Instagram to Fortnite and “sleep,” a mindset that fits naturally with the coming wave of AI‑generated video and interactive experiences.​ (In 2017, Netflix co-founder Reed Hastings famously said “sleep” was one of the company’s biggest competitors, as it was busy pioneering the binge-watch.) Grous also sounded a warning for the age of post-AI content: The binge-watch won’t feel as good anymore, and there will be some kind of backlash. As critics such as The New York Times‘ James Poniewozik increasingly note, streaming shows don’t seem to be as re-watchable as even recent hits from the golden age of cable TV, such as Mad Men. Grous said he sees a future where the endangered movie theater makes a comeback. “People are going to want to go outside and meet or go to the theater. Like, we’re not just going to want to be fed AI slop for 16 hours a day.” Editor’s note: the author worked for Netflix from June 2024 through July 2025.

headline: Software Architecture: How To Navigate A Polarized Tech Landscape
author: Barney Krishnan
content_snippet: Barney is a seasoned Data Executive at UniCredit with expertise in financial services, digital banking, AI and data modernization platforms. getty Software architecture has evolved significantly over the past 30 years. It began with monolithic applications running on centralized servers, progressi
full_content: Barney is a seasoned Data Executive at UniCredit with expertise in financial services, digital banking, AI and data modernization platforms. getty Software architecture has evolved significantly over the past 30 years. It began with monolithic applications running on centralized servers, progressing to multitier architectures that improved modularity and scalability. Growing system complexity led to service-oriented and component-based architectures emphasizing loose coupling and reuse. Agile methodologies emerged in the early 2000s, enabling iterative delivery and enhanced team collaboration. Today, Agile is central to organizations, driving continuous delivery and adaptability. Cloud adoption has matured into strategic multicloud and SaaS integration, especially within financial institutions, balancing open-source and custom platforms for flexibility and compliance. Artificial intelligence now plays a key role as collaborative agentic systems reshape development. The technology landscape is increasingly bipolar, defined by cutting-edge innovation on one side and legacy systems on the other. Architects navigate this divide, balancing technical debt with business and organizational goals. Shifting Architectural Paradigms: From Monolithic To Modular The traditional three-tier architecture, designed more than a decade ago, is increasingly regarded as legacy. The industry is now shifting toward lighter, more modular core platforms—featuring smart contracts and thin ledgers—that facilitate the seamless integration of auxiliary solutions. Yet, alongside these modern advancements, another reality exists: Roughly 70% of transaction processing still relies on mainframe systems. Although modern technologies have propelled progress in certain business functions, this entrenched legacy infrastructure presents significant modernization challenges, particularly in areas such as integration, data governance and quality. This dynamic recalls the early days of the Industrial Revolution, when machines were introduced into Britain’s cotton-spinning industry in the late 1700s and early 1800s. Initially, factory owners attempted to replace human spinners with machines directly, without reorganizing the factory workflow, resulting in minimal productivity gains. It was only through incremental innovations, reorganizing assembly lines and adopting larger-scale production that considerable productivity improvements were realized. From a design perspective, this era marked a shift not only in scale and approach but also in the fundamental “wiring” of the entire production process. The Changing Role Of Architecture In Today's SDLC In today’s software development life cycle (SDLC), architectural roles can sometimes be dismissed as either overly strategic for the rapid pace or irrelevant in the continuously evolving tech landscape. Yet, foundational architectural principles remain unchanged. What has transformed is the physical architecture—the tooling and technology underlying delivered solutions—which now evolves so rapidly that the challenge is less about speed and more about the fundamental approach to SDLC itself. This is not merely a matter of choosing new technologies or keeping pace, but about rethinking how applications are built and interconnected. The required architectural thinking must not be just bigger, faster or better, but fundamentally different. A Three-Pronged Framework For Modern Enterprise Architecture A well-structured architectural function segments the complex IT landscape into three key domains—legacy systems, modern platforms and transitional integrations—each requiring tailored approaches to balance stability, innovation and interoperability. 1. Legacy Systems: Critical workloads often run on mainframes or monolithic platforms. The focus is on ensuring uptime and stability with minimal capital spend while managing incremental innovations or phased functionality extraction. Architects must manage risk while preparing for potential modernization. 2. Modern Platforms: These include scalable, modular, cloud-native and AI-augmented solutions for digital-first users and operational workflows. Investments focus on building flexible, well-governed and future-proof platforms that integrate emerging technologies such as blockchain and smart contracts. 3. Transitional Integrations: This zone bridges legacy and modern systems through solutions that emphasize integration, data consistency and interoperability. The goal is to develop a clear modernization road map that ensures smooth data flow, governance and operational continuity until complete modernization is achieved. Integrating Architecture As Code Into The Three-Pronged Approach Architecture as code (AaC) is reshaping how firms manage and evolve their software architecture by defining architectural components, rules and configurations as executable, version-controlled code. This practice transforms architecture from static artifacts into living, automated models that evolve continuously alongside application development. Within the three-pronged architecture function, AaC plays a critical role: • In the legacy landscape, AaC supports incremental modernization by automating configuration management and ensuring stable integration points, reducing risk while preserving uptime. • For the modern landscape, AaC enables highly modular, scalable and governed platforms through code-driven architectural validation, automated governance and continuous compliance embedded in development pipelines. • In the transitional integration layer, AaC codifies and automates interfaces, data flows and dependencies, ensuring seamless interoperability between legacy and modern systems with traceability and alignment. By adopting architecture as code, organizations can bridge the gap between architectural vision and implementation, enhancing agility, reducing technical debt and enabling collaborative innovation involving both human engineers and AI agents. Operationalizing The Architecture Function In Large Enterprises Operationalizing architecture requires balancing centralization and decentralization depending on company culture, while ensuring principles are defined and enforced centrally. Clear, granular definitions of business and technical capabilities, coupled with precise technology mappings, remove ambiguity and enable consistency. Embedding architecture into Agile, DevOps and operational workflows facilitates continuous governance and automated compliance, turning strategy into measurable outcomes. AaC advances this by automating modeling and validation; bridging vision and execution across legacy, modern and transitional domains; and turning architecture into a key driver of innovation and operational excellence. In Summary As we advance through 2025 and beyond, software engineering and enterprise architecture are evolving toward embracing automation, modularity and intelligent collaboration. The increasing bipolarity of the technology landscape—juxtaposing legacy cores and modern, cloud-native ecosystems—demands differentiated architectural strategies balancing stability and agility. Emerging paradigms like architecture as code will continue to blur the lines between strategy and execution, embedding architecture deeply into CI/CD pipelines and empowering AI-assisted innovation. Enterprise architects will play a critical role in designing adaptable, composable and governed platforms that embrace cloud-native, AI-augmented and blockchain-enabled technologies. Succeeding in this environment requires new architectural mindsets that emphasize integration, automation and continuous modernization while respecting the operational realities of legacy systems. Firms that master this balance can unlock agility, resilience and lasting business value in an increasingly dynamic digital world.

headline: Microsoft Warning — Act Rapidly And Change Passwords As Attacks Strike
author: Davey Winder
content_snippet: Change your password now, Microsoft urges as Shai-Hulud worm attacks continue. NurPhoto via Getty Images In response to what the Microsoft Defender Security Research Team has called “one of the most significant cloud-native ecosystem compromises observed recently,” it has urged organizations to act
full_content: Change your password now, Microsoft urges as Shai-Hulud worm attacks continue. NurPhoto via Getty Images In response to what the Microsoft Defender Security Research Team has called “one of the most significant cloud-native ecosystem compromises observed recently,” it has urged organizations to act rapidly and replace passwords. Here’s what you need to know about the so-called Shai-Hulud 2.0 Dune Worm attacks. Forbes Microsoft And CISA Issue Critical New Alert, Windows Attacks Confirmed Microsoft Issues Critical Warning Following Shai-Hulud 2.0 Dune Worm Attacks On September 23, the Cybersecurity Infrastructure and Security Agency, which refers to itself as America’s Cyber Defense Agency, issued an urgent alert regarding a self-replicating worm, known as Shai-Hulud, targeting Application Programming Interface keys for cloud services such as Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud Platform, and Microsoft Azure. Fast-forward to now, and the Microsoft Defender Security Research Team has published new guidance for “detecting, investigating, and defending against the supply chain attack,” as Shai-Hulud 2.0 enters the cyber equation. “The Shai‑Hulud 2.0 campaign builds on earlier supply chain compromises,” Microsoft said, “but introduces more automation, faster propagation, and a broader target set.” This includes executing malicious code during the pre-install phase of the infected npm managed packages, which means that it happens before any security checks can be made. “Stolen credentials are exfiltrated to public attacker-controlled repositories,” the warning continued, “which could lead to further compromise.” This supply chain attack is, Adi Bleih, a security researcher for external risk management at Check Point, told me, unusually aggressive as a result. “By activating before installation completes and exfiltrating secrets into attacker-controlled GitHub repositories,” Bleih said, “the operators gained rapid access to significant volumes of cloud and developer credentials.” MORE FOR YOU Ken Johnson, chief technology officer of DryRun Security, meanwhile, confirmed that Shai-Hulud 2.0 is the third attack to have been attributed to a threat group identified as S1ngularity. “This second version of the Shai-Hulud worm tells us the attackers are refining their techniques and improving upon their previous mistakes,” Johnson advised. As such, it’s a “massively dangerous and disruptive campaign.” The Microsoft Defender Security Research Team mitigation recommendations are unequivocal: Rapidly rotate and revoke exposed credentials. Review the Key Vault assets on the critical asset management page and investigate any relevant logs for unauthorized access. Isolate affected CI/CD agents or workspaces. Prioritize high-risk attack paths to reduce further exposure. Remove unnecessary roles and permissions granted to identities assigned to CI/CD pipelines; specifically review access to key vaults. Don’t delay, absorb all the Microsoft advice and act rapidly as has been recommended. You know it makes sense.

headline: Google Confirms Free Upgrade For Millions Of Google Photos Users
author: Paul Monckton
content_snippet: Google Photos' latest update brings new video editing tools to Android and iOS GOOGLE Key Takeaways Google Photos has significantly upgraded its video editor The update reintroduces the text overlay tool removed in October New audio mixing controls let you balance original audio with background
full_content: Google Photos' latest update brings new video editing tools to Android and iOS GOOGLE Key Takeaways Google Photos has significantly upgraded its video editor The update reintroduces the text overlay tool removed in October New audio mixing controls let you balance original audio with background music Google Photos has received a significant overhaul of its video editing capabilities, continuing the app’s transition from a passive media library into an active content creation tool. The move follows the October launch of the app’s new video editor, which added several features but also removed key functions . Now, as revealed by Google Product Manager Nick Staubach, the company has responded to user requests by introducing five new video editing tools that could transform the way you use the app. We also see the partial return of one of those missing features. New Universal Timeline With Precision Editing The most significant addition is a new “Universal Timeline” that lets you edit together multiple clips in the Highlight Videos feature. The October update already allows you to string clips together and move them around, but the new Universal Timeline lets you trim individual clips precisely, apply adjustments and sync them up to a new audio track visible below the video clips. MORE FOR YOU It also features a new “Adaptive Canvas” that automatically adjusts the preview layout as you go. New Video Templates (Android First) This new feature lets you select from a range of pre-made video templates, which add stylized text and automatically cut your clips to the beat of a selected soundtrack. You can choose your favorite clips manually or tap “Help me select” to find appropriate content for your Highlight Video. Android users will find the templates in the Highlight Video creator, but iOS support is not available yet. Having removed the feature as part of the Markup tool, the new enhanced Text tool brings back the ability to overlay text on your videos. PAUL MONCKTON New Text Tool (Android First) The new Text tool restores the ability to add text overlays, a feature that went missing when Google removed the “Markup” function in the October update. The Text function is now available in the main video editor, but it’s easy to miss. Go to the function icons at the bottom of the screen, and you’ll find it tucked away on the far right. You can choose from a selection of fonts and colours, and you can position, resize and orient your text however you like. What’s still missing is the original Markup function's ability to overlay freehand lines and annotations on your video. Once again, iOS users will have to wait, as the Text feature is exclusive to Android for now. New Audio Mixing Controls After you’ve added music to your video clip, you can use the new volume control to adjust the mix between your original audio and the soundtrack. This small but vital feature makes the music feature usable in situations where the original audio might previously have been too quiet to hear over the added soundtrack. These new features represent a significant upgrade for Google Photos, enabling users to quickly create shareable content without leaving the app, without the added complexity of switching to a more powerful third-party editor like CapCut or Instagram Edits. The new Google Photos video-editing update is rolling out now, but you may need to wait for the features to be pushed to your account, and you may receive some features before others. Follow @paul_monckton on Instagram. Forbes Google Photos Adds New Video Editor But Removes Key Features

headline: Google’s New Android Update Locks Apps On Pixel And Samsung Phones
author: Zak Doffman
content_snippet: A major change for Android NurPhoto via Getty Images Google has come under fire for releasing upgrades and feature updates first on Pixel, leaving more popular Android phones behind. But those awkward optics are kind of reversed in the latest update to leak out of pre-release Android code. Android
full_content: A major change for Android NurPhoto via Getty Images Google has come under fire for releasing upgrades and feature updates first on Pixel, leaving more popular Android phones behind. But those awkward optics are kind of reversed in the latest update to leak out of pre-release Android code. Android Authority, which discovered this update in Android’s Canary 2512 release, says “Google may be working on a native App Lock feature for Android 17 that lets users secure specific apps without the restrictions of Private Space. This new API allows the default launcher to lock apps, eliminating the need to use third-party app lockers.” Forbes Google Issues Emergency Update For All 3 Billion Chrome Users—Attacks Confirmed The expectation is this will use the phone’s biometric unlock to protect apps, rather than introduce anything new and complicated. This functionality has been added to Android builds by some other OEMs and Apple has now brought the same to iPhone: “If you want to show someone something on your iPhone but want peace of mind that they can’t get into certain apps, you can lock or hide the apps.” This will be a major uplift for Samsung and Pixel owners. It’s not expected to launch as an Android 16 update, as we have seen with some other new security updates. Instead, Android Authority says it’s more likely to come next year with Android 17. That suggests another OS uplift that puts security and privacy first. MORE FOR YOU Locking apps in this way is much more usable than having to secure apps in Android’s Private Space or similar, taking them out of the phone’s normal UI. Instead, it just means that launching your photo album or note-taking app is safe from prying eyes. Forbes Do Not Install These Microsoft Teams Or Google Meet Updates Leaving your Pixel or Samsung with your kids — or even friends, colleagues or partner — is less nerve-wracking, when anything sensitive can be so easily secured. I suspect it’s a feature Pixel users will find themselves using a lot, without having to install third-party overlays. You’ll then be able to safely leave your phone behind without worrying.

headline: Google Issues Emergency Update For All 3 Billion Chrome Users—Attacks Confirmed
author: Zak Doffman
content_snippet: Update now warning for all Chrome users. dpa/picture alliance via Getty Images Just days after warning that Android is under attack, with two vulnerabilities now being exploited in the wild, Google has confirmed the same for Chrome. Another zero-day. Another warning attacks are underway. Another em
full_content: Update now warning for all Chrome users. dpa/picture alliance via Getty Images Just days after warning that Android is under attack, with two vulnerabilities now being exploited in the wild, Google has confirmed the same for Chrome. Another zero-day. Another warning attacks are underway. Another emergency update. Google Confirms Android Attacks—No Fix For Most Samsung Users “Google is aware that an exploit for 466192044 exists in the wild,” the company warned in its late Dec. 10 release. This is a high-severity vulnerability but a critical update. This doesn’t even have a CVE tag yet. Google just says it’s “under coordination.” Forbes Apple’s iPhone Security—Even Putin Can’t Beat iMessage For desktop users, “the Stable channel has been updated to 143.0.7499.109/.110 for Windows/Mac and 143.0.7499.109 for Linux.” Meanwhile, “ChromeOS version 16433.65.0 (Browser version 142.0.7444.234) has rolled out” and Google has also “just released Chrome 143 (143.0.7499.109) for Android.” MORE FOR YOU It’s usual for Google to keep “access to bug details and links restricted until a majority of users are updated with a fix. We will also retain restrictions if the bug exists in a third party library that other projects similarly depend on, but haven’t yet fixed.” Forbes FBI Warns All Smartphone Users—Stop Making These Calls But it’s unusual for a fix to be rushed out so quickly there are not even the usual classifications that enable the security industry to track updates and fixes. That tells us this emergency update moved from disclosure to release immediately, which suggests attacks may be more serious that a typical, targeted Chrome zero-day might suggest. If You See This Google Message, Your Gmail Is Under Attack In addition to the emergency fix, Google has also patched CVE-2025-14372, a “use after free in Password Manager” and CVE-2025-14373, an “inappropriate implementation in Toolbar.” Both of these were disclosed by external researchers. The password manager CVE is interesting, given recent concerns on browser-based password management. As ever, the new update with download automatically and then you will see a flag in your browser to restart. Do that immediately. Your normal tabs will restart but your private browsing “incognito” tabs will not. So save any work before you restart.

headline: 7 Legal Tech Trends That Will Reshape Every Business In 2026
author: Bernard Marr
content_snippet: Legal and compliance teams are entering one of the most technology-driven years the profession has ever seen. Adobe Stock Legal and compliance teams are entering one of the most technology-driven years the profession has ever seen. AI, automation and new regulatory pressures are converging, creatin
full_content: Legal and compliance teams are entering one of the most technology-driven years the profession has ever seen. Adobe Stock Legal and compliance teams are entering one of the most technology-driven years the profession has ever seen. AI, automation and new regulatory pressures are converging, creating a landscape where speed, accuracy and strategic insight determine which organisations stay ahead. According to the Thomson Reuters Future Of Professionals report, most experts already expect AI to transform their work within five years, with many viewing it as a positive force. The challenge now is clear: legal and compliance leaders must understand the tools reshaping their field and prepare their teams for a very different way of working in 2026. Here are the trends that will matter most. 1. AI Agents As Legal Assistants AI agents are a huge step forward from previous generations of information assistants like search engines and chatbots. This is because they can take action and work towards goals autonomously, instead of just answering questions and generating content. The implications of this are huge, as after delegating all the repetitive, manual work, human workers will have much more time for the more important tasks, often requiring interpersonal skills computers can’t match, that generate real value. 2. AI As A Driver Of Business Strategy The ability of AI to forecast risk, model the impact of regulatory changes, analyze markets and identify opportunities for efficiency is becoming central to business decision-making. Rather than simply delegating routine work, forward-looking professionals are increasingly looking towards new technology to drive innovation and deliver new business models. This could include offering subscription-based AI services to clients and new data-driven products. MORE FOR YOU 3. Automation In Judicial Administration Courts frequently suffer from long backlogs and shortages of trained human staff, a global problem that often delays the administration of justice. To tackle this, we can expect to see increasing use of AI and automation technology deployed as an administrative tool, assisting with scheduling, creating personalized summaries and procedural documents for judiciaries and court staff, as well as anticipating and predicting bottlenecks and delays. 4. Always-On Compliance Monitoring Regulatory intelligence platforms perform tasks involving monitoring for regulatory and legislative changes, providing live tracking and updates, and sometimes even automatically generating and filing reports to maintain compliance. This fast-growing field of regtech helps legal teams avoid falling foul of regulatory risks by flagging incoming requirements and tracking law changes across relevant jurisdictions. By making responding to regulatory change a managed, automated process, legal professionals will be better positioned to avoid unexpected surprises. 5. Cybersecurity As An Essential Survival Tool Law firms, with their huge troves of client data, represent prime targets for cyber criminals. This is demonstrated by stats telling us that over 40 percent of US firms experienced security breaches last year. This means that in 2026, security can no longer be solely on the agenda of the IT department. Firms that fail to implement robust defenses, safeguard confidentiality and build organization-wide security-first cultures risk huge penalties and, even more damagingly, loss of client trust. 6. Predictive Litigation Until now, AI use cases in law firms have centered around automation of repetitive workflows such as research and summarizing. This is likely to change as more sophisticated models open the door to probabilistic use cases such as predicting the outcome of litigation and assessing risk. The ability to more accurately estimate potential costs and rewards will mean law firms can set prices and manage cases more efficiently, as well as make better educated decisions about when to fight and when to settle. 7. Compliance As Part Of The Everyday Automation Fabric Today, software for carrying out everything from marketing automation to HR to AI-powered accounts reconciliation comes with compliance functionality “bolted on”. Although this will remove some manual and repetitive work from the schedule of legal and compliance professionals, there will still be a need to provide oversight, where employees who are not legal specialists could otherwise be handling sign-offs. I expect auditing the operation of compliance processes in end-to-end, non-specialist tools and platforms to form a growing part of the workload of legal and compliance professionals in 2026. As these technologies mature, the legal and compliance landscape will continue to shift from reactive oversight to proactive, intelligence-driven strategy. The firms and professionals who thrive in 2026 will be those who embrace these tools early, build the skills to use them well and position themselves as trusted partners in an increasingly complex business environment.

headline: Billionaire Cheng Family Selling Alinta Energy To Singapore’s Sembcorp For $4.3 Billion
author: Yessar Rosendar
content_snippet: The Alinta Wind Farm, south of Geraldton in Western Australia. AFR Picture by ERIN JONASSON (Photo by Fairfax Media via Getty Images via Getty Images) Fairfax Media via Getty Images Chow Tai Fook Enterprises—controlled by Hong Kong’s billionaire Cheng family —announced Thursday that it has agreed t
full_content: The Alinta Wind Farm, south of Geraldton in Western Australia. AFR Picture by ERIN JONASSON (Photo by Fairfax Media via Getty Images via Getty Images) Fairfax Media via Getty Images Chow Tai Fook Enterprises—controlled by Hong Kong’s billionaire Cheng family —announced Thursday that it has agreed to sell Australian power company Alinta Energy to Singapore’s Sembcorp Industries in a deal valued at A$6.5 billion ($4.3 billion). Singapore-listed Sembcorp will buy all the shares of Alinta from Chow Tai Fook Enterprises, according to joint-statement released on Thursday. The deal is subject to regulatory approval and is expected to be completed in the first half of 2026. “Our eight-year investment in Alinta has played an important role in providing reliable and affordable energy to Australians,” Henry Cheng, chairman of Chow Tai Fook Group said in the statement. “I am immensely proud of what the Chow Tai Fook Enterprises and Alinta teams have accomplished on the energy transition journey”. Sembcorp said the acquisition will help it reach its target of producing 25 gigawatts of renewable energy by 2028. Alinta currently produces 3.4 gigawatts of electricity generated from gas, wind, solar and coal plants across Australia and New Zealand and is developing another 10.4 gigawatts of additional. Sembcorp added that most of its planned S$14 billion ($10.80 billion) investment between now and 2028 will go toward renewable energy projects. The Cheng family has been selling assets to pay off mounting debts of their real estate firm New World Development, which has been hit by a property downturn in Hong Kong and China. Earlier this year, New World sold its hotel in the Philippines’ Makati financial district to Ayala Land for an undisclosed amount. Last year, it also sold a shopping mall in Hong Kong’s Tsuen Wan district to Chinachem Group for HK$4 billion ($510 million). MORE FOR YOU Adrian Cheng, the third-generation heir of the Cheng family, stepped down as CEO of New World in September last year after the company reported its first annual loss in 20 years. He has since launched a new firm that focuses on digital assets and “transformative industries” such as entertainment. With an estimated net worth of $19.5 billion, Henry Cheng and his family are among the wealthiest in Hong Kong. Henry succeeded his late father, Cheng Yu-tung, as chairman of Chow Tai Fook Jewellery Group and New World Development.

headline: Some Random Tips On Legal Brief Writing
author: Jay Adkisson
content_snippet: Good legal writing is a lot more than simply getting the law right. getty The year 2025 has been a very busy one for me in terms of drafting legal briefs on some issue or another. This means that not only was I writing briefs for my side, but I was also forced to carefully scrutinize the briefs of
full_content: Good legal writing is a lot more than simply getting the law right. getty The year 2025 has been a very busy one for me in terms of drafting legal briefs on some issue or another. This means that not only was I writing briefs for my side, but I was also forced to carefully scrutinize the briefs of my learned opponents. Hardly an hour went by that I did not see some legal writing technique or another that I thought could have been done better, if not much better. So, adding this year to my previous 35 as a lawyer, I humbly offer these few suggestions in no particular order as to how one can be a better brief writer. Oh, and the General Rule is in effect here: General rules are generally inapplicable. Which is to say that you have to use your good common sense and experience to know when these suggestions should be applied or not applied. Now let's proceed. Write your brief as if you were drafting the court's opinion. The courts implicitly tell us how they want briefs drafted. They do this by the form in which they publish their opinions. Thus, if you want to know how to properly draft a brief, it should be in basically the same form that courts write their opinions. The greatest honor to brief writing that can be bestowed is that of a court substantially copying parts of a brief as its own opinion. This is the standard to which you should aspire. This standard also dictates some of the following standards. Don't take shots at opposing parties or opposing counsel. Court opinions generally do not criticize parties or their counsel unless they have acted egregiously. Neither should you. Judges frequently lecture at continuing legal education events on things they hate to see in briefs. Shots at opposing parties or their counsel is usually at the top of this list. It doesn't make the other side look bad so much as it will make you look bad. The recoil is worse than the shot. If the opposing party or their counsel has done something stupid, you don't call it stupid. Instead, you set out what happened in a purely factual and perhaps even in a bland manner and let the court reach its own conclusion. Adjectives, adverbs and hyperbole. One of the most common drafting mistakes is the use of adjectives, adverbs and hyperbole to try to make something look better or worse than it would be described by a bland recitation of the facts or law. This forces the court to mentally blot out these things to try to understand what actually happened. If the court does not agree with your characterization, the credibility of your brief will suffer. Again, the recoil is worse than the shot ― a common theme in drafting. There is also something of a reverse-psychology going on with the court. A good example is the use of the word "simple". Say that you draft, "The issue here is simple." The first subconscious reaction of the judge will likely be to think that the issue is probably not simple at all. By using this one word, you may have disarmed the very point that you are trying to get across. This is where you want to adopt the Detective Joe Friday saying, "Just the facts, ma'am." When I'm self-editing my own briefs, a good part of this is in stripping out adjectives, adverbs and hyperbole that I've let slip in. Emphasis. Bad brief writing is frequently characterized by the overuse of emphasis: boldface, italics, and underlining. Some folks think they are writing a great brief because they are using lots of emphasis of the most important points. They are wrong. The use of emphasis is more often than not an unnecessary distraction. Use emphasis a lot and a brief becomes the legal version of your elder uncle's emails in ALL CAPS. If the volume is turned up everywhere, what might have been a beautiful symphony becomes traffic noise. We previously saw that commas are warning flags that a sentence is too long. Well, emphasis is a warning flag that a passage has been drafted poorly. If the passage was well-drafted, then you shouldn't need emphasize anything. A great brief uses no emphasis at all. Just look at court opinions: Judges rarely use emphasis. Neither should you. Emphasis might be necessary to emphasize an important word or phrase in a long quote, such as of a statute, but otherwise emphasis should be axed out in edited. MORE FOR YOU Flow Is Important. A great brief will have a great flow with each paragraph leading into the next. A bad brief is choppy and each paragraph seems divorced from every other. The latter briefing style is all too common. I have buddies who think they are great brief writers (indeed, they have told me so) because they can state facts and law in a logical order. Which is true as far as that goes, but their briefs are just an awful read because there is no flow. If somebody just copied headnotes in a logical fashion, it would not be too different from their briefs. This ultimately means that the judge must figure out for herself how all this stuff fits together. Very quickly, the judge will just want to tune it all out. A good flow results from thinking ahead to the next paragraph. What is a good way to transition to the next paragraph? Figure that out and the brief will have a nice flow. Run-on sentences. Everybody does this, including me. The error is in drafting a single long sentence which encompasses more than one thought. A run-on sentence frequently causes the court to stop and read the sentence over to get its drift. This disrupts the flow of the brief and the larger point you are trying to get across. Iti s usually easy to identify run-on sentences in editing because the sentence is just too long. But some more friendly advice: Commas are often a warning flag that the sentence is too long. You may have to use commas in then/if sentences. In most other occasions it is better to just stop the sentence and start a new one. Conjunctives are also warning flags as they may also indicate a sentence that should be bifurcated. Long paragraphs. The analog to the run-on sentence is the run-on paragraph. You know the kind. It starts at the top of page 11 and ends at the bottom of page 13. Courts typically do not write their opinions this way and neither should you write your briefs. The obvious problem with long paragraphs is that they cause ideas to be lost and jumbled. Here is a forest, and good luck finding the most important tree. Plus, just having a big block of unbroken text makes the reader's mind want to tune out. If a sentence should represent no more than a single thought, then a paragraph should bundle several thoughts together to make no more than a single idea. Starting a new paragraph tells the reader that discussion of the last idea has ended and a new idea is incoming. A good drafting trick, not to be overused, is to offer a single sentence as its own paragraph if you really want to emphasize that point. Another good rule of thumb is that a paragraph in a brief should be just three or four sentences, and rarely longer than five. Footnotes. The utility of footnotes is to include non-essential information that might or might not be useful to the reader. Unfortunately, footnotes are often overused. If information really is important, then it needs to be in the body of the brief. If it is not important, then it probably needs to be left out entirely. Briefs should generally not have more than one footnote every five pages or so. Using footnotes to try to save on page space is an inherently bad idea, since whatever bang you get from the footnote will be less than the drag on the flow of the brief. Note that when sparingly used, footnotes can be a great way to emphasize certain points ― if a brief only has one footnote, that by itself will call special attention to the footnote. String cites. String cites are a bunch of citations to authorities which say the same thing. With one exception, string cites are a bad idea. They don't accomplish anything and judges hate them. Some court rules even prevent such string citing. Use no more than three citations and then stop and ask yourself whether you really need the other two. Most of the time you don't. The exception is when the issue being briefed turns on a singular point of law. If that happens and the authorities are solid on the point, then feel free to plaster that singular point with a long string cite for emphasis. Or, if you are really feeling good about the point, just place a single citation in the body of the brief and then drop a footnote with a long string cite after the statement "Additional authorities include ....". Descriptive Headers. Why folks don't use descriptive headers I just don't understand. Which is better? "VI. Alter Ego" or "VI. Plaintiff Presents No Admissible Evidence Of The Elements Of Alter Ego"? A good header is like a tour guide: It tells the judge where you are going and what to look out for. A descriptive header may also allow the judge to skip over sections that she already agrees with, thus allowing for more time on the disputed points. There is also a credibility play: If your header promises X, and X is proven in the following text, then your credibility with the court goes up. This door can also swing both ways, of course, so a header should never promise something that it can't deliver. Finally, the use of descriptive headers also allows for a more useful table of contents insofar as the judge can scan the table of contents and see where your brief is going. Introductions. Honestly, I go back and forth on use of introductions and particularly about lengthy introductions. Sometimes they make sense, sometimes they don't. The decider usually comes down to two things: (1) whether I have the page space that allows for an introduction without cutting into the meat of my brief, and (2) whether I think the judge is diligent or lazy. A diligent judge will probably just skim over the introduction and get to the hard substance of the brief. A lazy judge may only read the introduction and little more. Thus, if I think the judge will be diligent in reading the entire brief, then I may have no introduction at all or perhaps a very cursory one. If I think the judge is lazy, then I may have a long introduction that includes all the important points that I want to get across. The downside of an introduction is that you may force the judge to basically much of the same material twice. This risks the judge only skimming the meat of your brief and missing important nuances that are not expressed in the introduction. Most court opinions only offer a brief introduction of maybe a half-dozen sentences that generally outlines what the matter is about. That should probably be your default position on introductions as well. Keep The Obvious Stuff Very Short. A big waste of the drafter's time and energy, and a common annoyance to judges, goes to drafting lengthy sections of the brief on obvious stuff. A good example is the standard to be applied on a motion for summary judgment. Trust me, the judge drafted summary judgment motions and responses when she was in private practice. She now sees summary judgments at least several times a week ― a lot more than you do. She knows all about the summary judgment standard and so you don't have to tell her. All you really need to do here is to tell the court enough so that the judge knows you're on the same page as to these standards. That could be a single well-drafted sentence, like "A motion for summary judgment tests whether the plaintiff can point to sufficient admissible evidence to establish a prima facie case as to each of his claims, see FRCP 56 and Wellington v. Napoleon." The point being that there is no need to drone on and on about a matter which the court already knows all about. Droning on and on about a subject suggests that you are unfamiliar with it and trying to find your way, as opposed to succinctly stating what is important and then moving which demonstrates your mastery of the subject. Avoid Clutter Issues. If I've learned one thing practicing law over 35 years, it is that most motions come down to no more than two or three important issues. Everything else is tangential. While folks have an emotional desire to try to respond to everything the other side said, that is not necessary. If something is not important, it normally should either be wholly ignored or treated sparingly. A common briefing mistake is to spend as much or more time on tangential issues than on important issues. Conversely, sometimes you can bait the other side into wasting precious briefing space on a tangential issue that you know is emotionally important to them but really doesn't amount to a hill of beans in the big scheme of things. Translate Complex Subjects Into Easy English. One might say that the real job of a lawyer is not so much to figure out complex legal issues, but rather to put those issues into a form that everybody can easily understand ― including the court. This is where the technically-correct but bad brief writers are sorted from the just as technically-correct but good brief writers. This skill doesn't come easily, but it does come with practice. It may take you several sentences to say in an easy way what otherwise could be said in a technical way in a single sentence, but the difference will be worth it. After all, the entire purpose of a brief is to get the court to fully understand one's position, not to risk the court missing the point because it was stated in a complex, albeit correct, fashion. Make it interesting. Legal briefs are usually dry and technical writings about a dry and technical subject. That does not mean, however, that legal briefs cannot be written in an interesting fashion ― or at least as interesting as they could be considering the subject matter. This requires literary style which is most easily acquired by reading. I enjoy reading financial histories, such as about the death of Lehman Brothers, because the authors of such works are able to take a relatively bland topic (there is fundamentally nothing sexy about the failure of an investment bank) and turn it into good reading. I'm also a strong believer that one cannot be a good writer unless they are also a prolific reader. This can be applied to legal writing as well. Compare "plaintiff fastened her seatbelt as was her custom and habit" against "Mary was a conscientious mother who was highly concerned about protecting her own personal well-being for the benefit of her family and thus fastened her seatbelt because that's the exact sort of thing that Mary did." Both statements are accurate, but the latter humanizes the plaintiff and is a more interesting read. Or compare "the Alvarez court overturned the Turner decision" with "by the time the Alvarez case rolled around, the Court of Appeals had realized that its decision in Turner was no longer tenable". Both are accurate, but the first is bland and the second is a more interesting way of saying the same thing. The more you read outside of the law, the better chance that you will have of picking up these literary skills and being able to apply them in your briefs. Appearance Is Important. I am a strong believer that a brief which cosmetically looks good will be more appealing to the court than one which looks common. What happens is that law firms and individual lawyers do not update their templates and thus end up using motion forms from the age of typewriters. One can quickly spot these briefs because they have right margin lines (typewriter stops) that are no longer required by the local rules, or they use an older font that is difficult to read. The use of Arial and Courier New fonts in briefs should be made a felony offense, but I still see these all the time. It conveys an air of laziness to the court. There are whole books on the use of fonts and making documents appear cleaner to create a better first impression with the court. Buy one and read it. Tables Of Content And Authorities. The local rules usually require that a brief exceeding a certain number of pages be accompanied by a table of contents and table of authorities. You should consider using them for shorter briefs as well ― my rule of thumb is usually 10 pages. Not only are tables of contents useful to the court, but they can also act as an introduction of sorts if you have used descriptive headers. And why not? Usually, such tables do not apply against the allowable page-count of the brief. It usually takes only a few minutes to include these tables, but they can be worth their weight in gold. Edit At Least Once From The Bottom Up. Starting from the end of a brief and then working towards the start is a very useful trick. This method makes you look at each sentence in isolation to see whether it makes sense standing on its own. Also, errors that you might have missed in a top-down edit can really stand out in a bottom-up edit. Anyway, these are some of my own thoughts about legal drafting. I hope they will be as of much use to you as they have been to me over the years.

headline: Get A Ringside View On Giti Tire’s EV-Powered Race To Catch Up With The World's Leading Tiremakers
author: Naazneen Karmali
content_snippet: Forbes Asia December Issue Forbes Asia A s an eventful 2025 draws to a close, it’s worth reflecting on its biggest moments. Few pundits could have predicted that this year would bring us a new spectator sport: trade policy. The muscular tariff regime of Trump 2.0 unleashed turmoil that governments
full_content: Forbes Asia December Issue Forbes Asia A s an eventful 2025 draws to a close, it’s worth reflecting on its biggest moments. Few pundits could have predicted that this year would bring us a new spectator sport: trade policy. The muscular tariff regime of Trump 2.0 unleashed turmoil that governments and businesses around the world scrambled to address. Some were better prepared than others, such as the protagonist of our cover story, former medical doctor Enki Tan, now executive chairman of Singapore-headquartered Giti Tire, a tiremaker with Indonesian roots. Tan’s global ambitions for Giti (pronounced “GT”) spurred him to invest $560 million in building a U.S. factory in 2017 to be closer to the company’s American customers. In another prescient move, Tan went all out to sharpen Giti’s technological edge in a growing segment of the market: tires for electric vehicles. In September, Chinese EV giant BYD’s new electric hypercar, sporting Giti’s wheels, notched a speed record of nearly 500 kilometers per hour to become the world’s fastest production car. As Tan disclosed to our contributing editor Ardian Wibisono, tires these days are loaded with much more than meets the eye: “I have people telling me, ‘Hey, they’re very easy to make. You just pour the rubber inside the mold and then the tire comes out.’ It’s not so easy.” Indeed, it wasn’t an easy year either for the tycoons on the accompanying list of Indonesia’s 50 Richest as a spell of civil unrest marked by street protests made investors skittish. Despite the uncertainty, the benchmark stock index was up by double-digits, resulting in the combined wealth of the country’s richest, ably compiled by our wealth team, crossing $300 billion. Halter founder and CEO Craig Piggott Adam Bove Scaling up to the billion-dollar valuation mark is an uncommon feat for companies in New Zealand. One among a handful of Kiwi unicorns is agritech firm Halter, profiled by our reporter Catherine Wang. Halter’s founder and CEO Craig Piggott, who grew up on his parents’ dairy farm in Matamata, a town immortalized as a filming location of Lord of the Rings, created a solar-powered smart collar for cows to help farmers manage their herds through virtual fencing technology. An alum of our 30 Under 30 Asia list of young achievers (class of 2021), Piggott is looking further afield to the U.S. as Halter’s next growth market. Forbes Asia’s 2025 Heroes Of Philanthropy Illustrations by Marco Lawrence for Forbes Asia In a month that marks the season of giving, we present a selection of Asia-Pacific’s notable givers in our annual Heroes of Philanthropy list. This unranked group, curated by a team led by editorial director Rana Wehbe Watson, spotlights wealthy individuals and families backing worthy causes, such as supporting young women studying science, technology, engineering and mathematics. To round up the philanthropy theme is a stateside story about billionaire couple Cari Tuna, a former journalist, and her husband, Facebook cofounder Dustin Moskovitz, who are giving away the bulk of their multibillion-dollar fortune as fast as they can. Their charity, Coefficient Giving, backs a range of causes, such as AI safety research. As the year turns, a heads-up about what we should brace for: Speed of Change, the theme of our Forbes Global CEO Conference to be held in Singapore in the fourth quarter of 2026. As always comments welcome at executiveeditor@forbesasia.com.

headline: Tariffs Drive New U.S. Solar Panel Production—Will Texas Miss Out?
author: Ed Hirs
content_snippet: Solar power plant in Central Texas getty Texas Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick has asked for a legislative inquiry into two companies that are constructing large scale solar panel manufacturing plants in Texas in reaction to a FOX News report that the companies have links to China and Chinese government offic
full_content: Solar power plant in Central Texas getty Texas Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick has asked for a legislative inquiry into two companies that are constructing large scale solar panel manufacturing plants in Texas in reaction to a FOX News report that the companies have links to China and Chinese government officials. While Patrick’s ultimate goal is not clear, he and other state officials who have been quick to criticize should think about the long-term needs of the state and the nation. Limiting solar panel manufacturing will prove short-sighted indeed. Ignoring for a moment that these companies are publicly traded in the U.S., the fact that they are investing hundreds of millions of dollars to build these facilities anywhere in the U.S. is a direct response to the 50% tariffs on Chinese solar cells to encourage U.S. based production. Tariffs give a massive economic incentive to foreign companies to invest and build manufacturing plants in the U.S. In 2003, Toyota broke ground on its light truck plant in Texas in response to U.S. trade policy that imposed a 25% import tariff on light trucks. Toyota now employs thousands of Texans and has invested billions of dollars in the state. So, it is not surprising that the first movers for solar cell and solar panel manufacturing in Texas would have ties to the world’s largest manufacturers that are based in China. Those companies have the state-of-the-art technology required to scale up production of more efficient solar cells—taking the 19th century invention well beyond the first practical applications of Bell Labs in the 1950s and improving on NASA’s well known use. As a practical solution to meeting exploding electricity demand in Texas and across the nation, the U.S. needs more solar panels - yesterday. To protect U.S. energy security, the manufacturing of these components should be sited in the U.S. The Electric Reliability Council of Texas announced that more than 225 gigawatts of large load projects have lined up to become new electricity customers in Texas. (For perspective, the Hoover Dam produces two gigawatts of electricity, so think of 112 Hoover Dams.) If actual demand reaches only one-third of that projected need, the average daily demand on the ERCOT grid will double. With at least a five-year backlog for new natural gas power plants and an even longer time horizon for permitting and building small nuclear power plants, the solution for Texas will be a very rapid buildout of solar farms and batteries. One of the companies that caught Patrick’s attention is T1 Energy, which is traded on the New York Stock Exchange. The company is mounting the largest effort to expand solar in Texas by investing more than $400 million in a new manufacturing plant in Austin, Texas. In total, the company expects to add more than 3,000 jobs in its Austin and Dallas operations alone. Vice President Vance recently met with T1 Chairman and CEO Dan Barcelo to discuss American energy and manufacturing – something he likely would not have done if he viewed T1 as a threat to the U.S. MORE FOR YOU America is playing catchup and, unfortunately, has already ceded the rest of the global solar panel market to China. Domestically, Texas is behind Ohio and Louisiana in manufacturing panels, and both of those states are hosting companies with ties to China. Texas does not have a monopoly on tax breaks or a skilled workforce. It had to hurt elected leaders in Austin that President Trump announced more than $92 billion of investments in new AI data centers and other electricity intensive businesses at the Pennsylvania Energy Summit earlier this year while pointing out that Texas did not win the investments. Does Texas really want to create a hostile environment and lose its position as Energy Capital of the World? More crucially, could Texas cost the U.S. the war on AI development against China? Texas leaders must think about the long game for the Lone Star State and welcome the reshoring of American technology.

headline: Rivian Adds Lidar Sensor To Upcoming R2 EV For Autonomous Driving
author: Sasha Lekach
content_snippet: Rivian will include a lidar sensor in its new electric SUV, the R2. Sasha Lekach For improved autonomous driving, electric automaker Rivian is finally adding lidar. At the EV startup’s Autonomy & AI Day at its Palo Alto, California offices, Rivian announced the introduction of lidar equipment into
full_content: Rivian will include a lidar sensor in its new electric SUV, the R2. Sasha Lekach For improved autonomous driving, electric automaker Rivian is finally adding lidar. At the EV startup’s Autonomy & AI Day at its Palo Alto, California offices, Rivian announced the introduction of lidar equipment into its sensor suite. As part of the Thursday event, CEO RJ Scaringe and top executives unveiled an in-house silicon chip for its Gen 3 autonomy platform for the lidar sensor to help power hands-free advanced driving. “It’s a massive leap forward with R2 vehicles,” Scaringe said on stage to kick off the day. The lidar sensor will be an option for the upcoming R2 smaller electric SUV due on the streets in 2026. The current all-electric Rivian models, R1T and R1S, only have camera and radar sensors. By the end of 2026 the sensor suite on the R2 will include 11 cameras, five radars and one lidar. Rivian declined to disclose its sensor suppliers. Rivian adds Lidar to its sensing system for autonomous driving. Rivian/Screenshot Rivian execs said this is the first time that a third sensor is part of the mix. Now that lidar costs have dropped and it can be integrated into the body of the car (it doesn’t sit on top of the car like a Waymo robotaxi) with a 120-degree field of view for the car to see, it makes sense to include it and have more robust data for autonomous features. Rivian put its lidar unit at the top of the windshield, where it’s barely noticeable—especially from the side. An Rivian R2 model shows where all the sensors, including the new lidar, will be placed in the car. Sasha Lekach MORE FOR YOU Lidar, which stands for LIght Detection And Ranging, uses light pulses to “see.” Vidya Rajagopalan, Rivian’s head of hardware, explained that lidar is much better in the dark and provides a 3D view of the world. It’s part of a new Gen 3 hardware and software system that will debut in the R2. Rivian’s Autonomy Platform updates will extend to Gen 2 owners with 3.5 million miles of roads eligible in the U.S. and Canada (up from the current 135,000 miles). It will function off-highway on roads with clear markings. So a lesser version of Tesla’s Full-Self Driving mode, but an improvement from the current Rivian Driver+ advanced driver assistance system. The Lidar sensor is integrated into the Rivian EV. Rivian Autonomy+ will be available as a subscription with over-the-air updates for a one-time free of $2,500 or $49.99 per month. Eventually, Scaringe and the team anticipates Level 4 eyes-off, hands-off driving capability with no human intervention from the start to end of a drive. The new sensor and improved autonomous driving experience joins other AI updates, including the Rivian Assistant, which replaces the existing the Amazon Alexa-based voice assistant in the car. This update will be available to all Rivian owners with both Gen 1 and Gen 2 hardware in the new year and works with Google Calendar, messaging and navigation, but will eventually work with more third-party apps. “It’s not just another chatbot,” Pranil Vora, senior manger for AI Platform at Rivian, said while speaking with the car conversationally. The Rivian Assistant uses regular conversation to turn on car functions. Rivian There’s about to be a lot more, “Hey, Rivian," especially if the R2 fulfills its expectation to increase sales for the EV maker with a more approachable (under $60,000) compact SUV.

headline: How AI Could Unlock Demand For Work
author: Rich Karlgaard
content_snippet: GettyImages Technologies disrupting the labor market will also bring job opportunity. Spend enough time in today’s AI debate and you’ll hear dire predictions of massive job losses. The World Bank says over one-third of Philippine jobs are at risk from AI and suggests similar job destruction in Asi
full_content: GettyImages Technologies disrupting the labor market will also bring job opportunity. Spend enough time in today’s AI debate and you’ll hear dire predictions of massive job losses. The World Bank says over one-third of Philippine jobs are at risk from AI and suggests similar job destruction in Asian middle-income countries like Indonesia and Malaysia. This view misses the underlying economic reality. AI is going to transform the global economy faster than many expect. But that’s not the same as upending it. History points toward a far more nuanced outcome: efficiency gains tend to unlock suppressed demand for work, not eliminate it. The Radiologist Lesson In his YouTube clip “What Everyone Is Getting Wrong About AI and Jobs,” Garry Tan, CEO of Y Combinator, an American technology accelerator and venture capital firm, says AI skeptics should look at a case study from a field once considered ground zero for AI’s looming disruption: radiology. In 2016, Geoffrey Hinton—one of the pioneers of neural networks—famously declared that “people should stop training radiologists now.” Within five years, he predicted, deep learning would outperform humans. Goodbye, radiologists. That was nearly a decade ago. Today, radiologists are not only still employed—their services are in record demand, says Tan. This despite an explosion of AI imaging tools that can detect and classify dozens of conditions faster and often more accurately than humans. Rather than eliminating radiologists, AI made them more productive—and demand expanded to match that new productivity. This dynamic is not new, explains Tan. Economists know it as Jevons Paradox, a 19th-century observation that efficiency gains often increase consumption rather than reduce it. Examples abound: MORE FOR YOU • Containerization in the 1960s eventually cut shipping costs by more than 90% and global trade exploded. Entire industries—logistics, freight forwarding, port automation—grew from what had been a cost-constrained bottleneck. • Cloud computing in the 2010s made infrastructure 10x cheaper. Traditional IT roles didn’t vanish; they evolved. Server admins became cloud architects managing environments at previously unimaginable scale. • AI inference today has become dramatically more efficient, and instead of dampening demand, it has ignited a global GPU frenzy—pushing Nvidia’s valuation to historic highs. What This Means for AI and Labor Tan continues: As AI lowers the marginal cost of analysis, drafting, coding, summarizing and decision support, we should expect more—never less—demand for the domains around those tasks. That doesn’t mean jobs remain unchanged. Many roles—especially those defined by repetitive, low-context tasks—will be refactored. Andrej Karpathy, a cofounder of OpenAI, has argued that AI will first transform highly structured jobs: customer support, data entry and high-volume administrative tasks. Yet even there, the likely outcome is not eradication but elevation. Humans become managers of AI workflows, supervisors of fleets of agents and quality-assurance leads rather than mechanical task executors. Businesses should draw two conclusions. First, the AI wave is real and accelerating, emphasizes Tan. Underestimating it is as dangerous today as dismissing the internet was in the late 1990s. Cost curves are falling, capabilities are compounding and competitive gaps are widening. Second, expecting an imminent collapse in labor demand is a misunderstanding of how economies respond to efficiency. AI will reshape the workforce, but through augmentation, new categories and reconfigured value chains—not mass idling of human capital. The companies that win in the next decade won’t be those that wait for permission or clarity. They will be those that recognize latent demand, design for hybrid human-AI workflows, and build the organizational muscle to scale them. Marginal Gains Forbes Great Plains Presidential Dark Horse — Doug Burgum Forbes The Great AI Deflation Bomb

headline: Smart Collars For Cows: How A 31-Year-Old Is Transforming Cattle Farming
author: Cat Wang, Yue Wang, Jessica Tan, Forbes Asia Team
content_snippet: New Zealand unicorn Halter, founded by Forbes 30 Under 30 Asia alum Craig Piggott, is one of the fastest-growing players in the virtual fencing market. A bout 100 tiny, moving yellow dots are clustered within a triangle on the screen of Craig Piggott’s mobile phone. Each dot represents a cow on his
full_content: New Zealand unicorn Halter, founded by Forbes 30 Under 30 Asia alum Craig Piggott, is one of the fastest-growing players in the virtual fencing market. A bout 100 tiny, moving yellow dots are clustered within a triangle on the screen of Craig Piggott’s mobile phone. Each dot represents a cow on his family’s dairy farm in New Zealand’s rural Waikato region, and each cow is wearing a wireless smart collar made of thick, black plastic, resembling a belt with a solar-panel-fitted buckle. Standing nearby, Piggott taps and holds a button on his phone screen. In an instant, the cows lift their heads and dutifully plod across the field to where fresh grass is ready for grazing. The bucolic scene is part of a video demonstration by Auckland-based Halter, Piggott’s nine-year-old company, to show how cows can be trained and managed with its virtual fencing technology. Relying primarily on vibrations and sound cues, Halter’s smart collars allow farmers, by using the app, to steer their cows toward new sections of a pasture and keep them within set boundaries. The 31-year-old chief executive tells Forbes Asia by a video call from San Francisco, where his lead VC investor is based, that his product saves between 20 and 40 hours of labor a week: “Our core milestone is building the category to a point where you just wouldn’t dream of running a farm or ranch without some form of virtual fencing product.” An alum of the 2021 Forbes 30 Under 30 Asia list, Piggott is on a mission to help farmers maximize every square kilometer of their land. Halter is one of the fastest-growing players in the nascent virtual fencing market. Its collars are used by 1,300 dairy and beef farms in New Zealand, Australia and the U.S. to manage nearly 650,000 cows, with 809,300 kilometers of virtual fencing put up to date. New Zealand, home to more cows than people, accounts for over three-fourths of its customer base. “We think lifting the productivity of the world’s land mass has got to be one of the most important and impactful problems to solve.” Halter is now zeroing in on the U.S., one of the world’s largest beef and dairy producers. Since it opened an office in Colorado last year, more than 200 American farmers in 22 states have virtually fenced over 39,400 kilometers using Halter's technology. The next stops on the company's three to five-year roadmap: the U.K., Ireland, Argentina and Brazil. “Agriculture covers half the world’s habitable land mass,” Piggott says. “We think lifting the productivity of the world’s land mass has got to be one of the most important and impactful problems to solve.” MOO-VING UP Halter’s collars are guiding cows to greener fields across New Zealand, Australia and now the U.S. Source: Halter Investors concur. In June, Halter raised $100 million at a $1 billion valuation in a series D funding round led by San Francisco-headquartered venture capital firm BOND, turning the company into one of New Zealand’s rare unicorns. “We live in this world where your head is on a swivel from the sheer number of AI companies going after every horizontal software opportunity,” says San Francisco-based Daegwon Chae, general partner at BOND, which has also invested in Canva, OpenAI and Revolut. “But farming and ranching are actually one of those very gargantuan markets just waiting for innovation.” In a global agritech market that’s expected to be worth $62 billion by 2030, per Boston-based consultancy BCG, Chae reckons Halter has an edge over its competitors. “If Halter is like a Tesla, some of these existing virtual fences are like diesel buggies from the ‘50s,” he says, adding that its software and app “feel native for the next generation of very mobile-savvy ranchers.” Piggott, who grew up on his parents’ dairy farm outside Matamata, a bustling town known to Middle Earth fans as a filming site for Lord of the Rings, understands the growing pressures faced by farmers. His parents would start their day at 4 a.m. and work over 100 hours a week. Aside from physical exhaustion, farmers face challenges to profitability that range from persistent labor shortages to high material and compliance costs. “Farming, in hindsight, was an awesome training ground for running a startup,” says Piggott. “You’re doing long hours, it’s seven days a week, and you’re not always in control, like if the weather’s bad or if you have a sick cow…I think that teaches you this level of resilience and this level of grit.” Craig Piggott at Brice Lewis Ranch in Salida, Colorado. The cow is sporting the latest generation of Halter collars, the P5. Adam Bove Shortly after graduating from the University of Auckland in 2016 with a bachelor’s degree in mechanical engineering, he landed a job at Rocket Lab’s factory in Auckland. At the time, the New Zealand-born space giant was finding its feet after setting up operations in California and still over a year away from its first successful orbital launch. The young Piggott was one of Rocket Lab’s “go-getters” with a knack for problem-solving, according to Rocket Lab’s billionaire founder and CEO, Peter Beck. “Craig was one of those folks who it was pretty obvious that if he turned his mind to something, he would be successful,” says Beck. Less than a year after joining, Piggott left to launch Halter with a friend from university (who later left the company), getting Beck on board as an angel investor and mentor with Stephen Tindall, founder of Kiwi retailer The Warehouse Group, and local VC firm Icehouse Ventures chipping in. “I’m not an expert in livestock. I know the tail end from the eating end, but that’s about it. But Craig was very clearly able to articulate a big opportunity,” says Beck. CASH COW Halter has raised more than $180 million to fuel its expansion. Excluding angel investors and grants Source: Halter, Crunchbase Piggott worked closely with a handful of farmers to improve on his initial concept of GPS collars. The first big challenge was creating a solar panel that fit on the band; next, ensuring that it wouldn’t break. Realizing that the collars needed to withstand significant daily wear and tear, Piggott and his team opted to use a type of bulletproof glass. Their first winter was spent redeveloping the battery to ensure it would stay charged on days where there was less sun to power it up. Halter’s collar was intended for dairy cows, primarily to herd them to milking sheds; new software for beef cattle came a few years later, which allowed for grazing control over larger areas. Cows are given directional signals through audio and vibrations that increase in frequency. For those that ignore repeated prompts, training is reinforced by a low-energy electric pulse, a fraction of the shock delivered by an electric fence, says the company, which outlines its animal welfare standards on its website. It takes between two to three days on average to get a cow up to speed. Halter's app and accompanying collar. Courtesy of Halter In addition to monitoring herd movements, Halter collars continuously collect health data from the cows, such as temperature readings and chewing patterns, which are fed to the company’s proprietary machine learning algorithms—dubbed “cowgorithms”—to predict disease and peak times for mating. Halter offers four types of subscription packages across dairy and beef to cover remote shifting, pasture-management services and animal health. Monthly rates, which depend on the size of a farm, its location and the herd size, start at NZ$9.90 ($5.70) per collar. Subscribers also need to purchase six-meter-tall transmission towers, priced at around NZ$7,800 each (a tower covers about an 8-kilometer radius or more, depending on the terrain). Dean McHardy, general manager and stud master at Tangihau Station—a sprawling 6,650-hectare Gisborne ranch near the North Island’s eastern coast that’s home to 1,550 cows—describes the product as “an absolute game changer.” Being able to move the herd and adjust grazing plans instantly, without needing to send people out, has been critical to manage feeding, he says, adding that pastures grazed off properly regrow higher-quality feed for the next rotation. McHardy chose Halter’s “Beef Class” package for the farm’s 350-strong Angus stud herd, which runs at NZ$8 per collar each month. He's set up five towers so far and plans to add four more: “Halter allows us to graze large, steep paddocks evenly without the need to install new fences that might not withstand the following winter or a cyclone." Adam Bove Piggott is now focused on scaling up in the U.S., the world’s largest beef producer, which according to the U.S. Department of Agriculture accounts for 20% of global production. While expanding operations there, Halter's been busy talking to farm owners unfamiliar with the technology. In the “tight-knit” world of farming, “the single best thing you can do is have a rancher or a farmer hear about the product or see the product from someone they trust,” says Piggott. The company says to date, it’s helped Halter’s American customers save $220 million over conventional fencing costs. In August, Halter announced a partnership with the U.S. Bureau of Land Management and its charitable arm, the Foundation for America’s Public Lands, which includes $2.7 million in funding to support ranchers using Halter collars on agency-managed land. Among the first takers is Cotoni-Coast Dairies, whose cattle graze on park land in California. Halter’s also looking for growth across the Tasman. Virtual fencing is allowed in three of Australia’s six states, and now Victoria and New South Wales, the country’s largest and second-largest dairy-producing states, respectively, are developing regulations for the technology, expected by the end of this year; South Australia is considering a similar move. Since its country launch in Tasmania two years ago, Halter says its collars are now found on a third of the dairy cows in the state. “Farming, in hindsight, was an awesome training ground for running a startup. You’re doing long hours, it’s seven days a week, and you’re not always in control” Piggott declines to comment on worldwide profit and revenue, adding that the company’s “main focus is on trying to scale our impact.” A filing to the New Zealand Companies Office shows subscription income in New Zealand grew 45% to NZ$35.9 million in the year ended March. After-tax profit reached NZ$53.8 million on NZ$71.8 million in revenue, which fell 20% from a year earlier. Halter says the drop reflects a lower level of support needed from its San Francisco-based parent company, Halter USA, which was established in 2017. (Its first U.S.-based investor, Chicago-headquartered Promus Ventures, participated in the startup’s seed round that year and has joined every round since.) The Kiwi firm faces some competition from other agritech players. Virtual fencing rivals include Nofence, a Norwegian company that raised $35 million in a series B funding round in September to expand in the U.S. and Europe; New Zealand-based farming equipment maker Gallagher’s eShepherd tools; and Vence, which was acquired by pharmaceutical giant Merck’s animal health subsidiary in 2022. For cow monitoring and health, there’s Merck’s Allflex, which offers tagging and tracking devices; CowManager, an ear tag-based monitoring system from the Netherlands; and smaXtec, an Austrian animal-health data provider. So far, no collars can replicate Halter’s full range of features, including its scale of data analysis, says Samantha Wong, a partner at Australian VC Blackbird Ventures who oversaw multiple investments in the company. “Their technical advantage is now so far ahead of the competition,” says Wong, “it’s Halter’s game to lose.” MORE FROM FORBES Forbes The $700 Million Chinese Robot Startup That Wants To Take On Tesla Forbes How A Small Singapore Biotech Found A Place Among Industry Giants

headline: Many State Attorneys General Warn Via Policy Letter That ‘Move Fast And Break Things’ Is Wrong When AI Is Adversely Impacting Our Mental Health
author: Lance Eliot
content_snippet: State AGs post a bruising letter to AI makers about getting their act together on dealing with AI that is undermining human mental health. getty In today’s column, I examine a newly released policy letter by numerous state-level attorneys general that brashly says AI makers need to up their game wh
full_content: State AGs post a bruising letter to AI makers about getting their act together on dealing with AI that is undermining human mental health. getty In today’s column, I examine a newly released policy letter by numerous state-level attorneys general that brashly says AI makers need to up their game when it comes to how their AI is impacting societal mental health. The classic techie adage of moving fast and breaking things is not suitable for matters involving national mental health. Period, end of story. Actually, there is a lot more to the story in the sense that the policy letter provides sixteen specific changes or practices that the AGs would like to see implemented. It is unclear what will happen if the AI makers do not adopt the stated indications. A vague sense of foreboding is simply that the law will be looking over their shoulders and that the listed aspects are intended to put the AI makers on general notice of what is expected of them. The mainstay is that, on a legal basis, there seems to be a lot of loosey-goosey cooked into this. As I will discuss, some of the points are already being done, while some are not. Some are being done in a shallow fashion, some are being done deeply. The wiggle room comes to bear in that the precise legal meaning is open to interpretation. Only once these disconcerting matters land in our courtrooms will it be more apparent as to the exact nature and scope involved. Let’s talk about it. This analysis of AI breakthroughs is part of my ongoing Forbes column coverage on the latest in AI, including identifying and explaining various impactful AI complexities (see the link here). AI And Mental Health As a quick background, I’ve been extensively covering and analyzing a myriad of facets regarding the advent of modern-era AI that produces mental health advice and performs AI-driven therapy. This rising use of AI has principally been spurred by the evolving advances and widespread adoption of generative AI. For a quick summary of some of my posted columns on this evolving topic, see the link here, which briefly recaps about forty of the over one hundred column postings that I’ve made on the subject. MORE FOR YOU There is little doubt that this is a rapidly developing field and that there are tremendous upsides to be had, but at the same time, regrettably, hidden risks and outright gotchas come into these endeavors, too. I frequently speak up about these pressing matters, including in an appearance last year on an episode of CBS’s 60 Minutes, see the link here.
